sourceQuestion:
Consider the regular grammar:
  S -> Xa | Ya
  S -> Za
  S -> epsilon
  W -> Wa
  W -> Sa
where S is the starting symbol, the set of terminals is {a} and the set of non-terminals is {S, W, X, Y, Z}. We wish to construct a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) to recognize the same language. What is the minimum number of states required for the DFA?
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 5

parsed.stem:
Consider the regular grammar:
  S -> Xa | Ya
  S -> Za
  S -> epsilon
  W -> Wa
  W -> Sa
where S is the starting symbol, the set of terminals is {a} and the set of non-terminals is {S, W, X, Y, Z}. We wish to construct a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) to recognize the same language. What is the minimum number of states required for the DFA?

sourceQuestion:
In the automaton below, q is the start state and f is the only final state. Consider the strings u1 = aba, u2 = bab, u3 = abba. Which of the following statements is true?

[The automaton has states q, r, s, f with transitions:
 q --a--> r, q --b--> s
 r --a--> q, r --b--> f
 s --a--> f, s --b--> q
 f --a--> s, f --b--> r]

A. The automaton accepts u1 and u2 but not u3
B. The automaton accepts each of u1, u2 and u3
C. The automaton rejects each of u1, u2 and u3
D. The automaton accepts u1 but u2 and rejects u1 and u3
sourceAnswer: B. The automaton accepts each of u1, u2 and u3

parsed.stem:
In the automaton below, q is the start state and f is the only final state. Consider the strings u1 = aba, u2 = bab, u3 = abba. Which of the following statements is true?

[The automaton has states q, r, s, f with transitions:
 q --a--> r, q --b--> s
 r --a--> q, r --b--> f
 s --a--> f, s --b--> q
 f --a--> s, f --b--> r]

options.text: The automaton accepts u1 and u2 but not u3
The automaton accepts each of u1, u2 and u3
The automaton rejects each of u1, u2 and u3
The automaton accepts u1 but u2 and rejects u1 and u3

sourceQuestion:
For a state machine with the following state diagram, the expression for the next state S' in terms of the current state S and the input variables x and y is:

[State diagram: States 0 and 1. From state 0: input x=0,y=0 -> stay 0; x=0,y=1 -> stay 0; x=1,y=0 -> go 1; x=1,y=1 -> go 1. From state 1: input x=0,y=0 -> go 0; x=0,y=1 -> stay 1; x=1,y=0 -> go 0; x=1,y=1 -> stay 1.]

A. S' = S'.y + S.x
B. S' = S'.y + S.x
C. S' = S.y + S'.x
D. S' = S.y + S'.x^2

parsed.stem:
For a state machine with the following state diagram, the expression for the next state S' in terms of the current state S and the input variables x and y is:

[State diagram: States 0 and 1. From state 0: input x=0,y=0 -> stay 0; x=0,y=1 -> stay 0; x=1,y=0 -> go 1; x=1,y=1 -> go 1. From state 1: input x=0,y=0 -> go 0; x=0,y=1 -> stay 1; x=1,y=0 -> go 0; x=1,y=1 -> stay 1.]

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following DFA in which S_0 is the start state and S_1, S_3 are the final states.

[DFA with 4 states S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3 over alphabet {x, y}:
- From S_0: on x -> S_1, on y -> S_2
- From S_1: on x -> S_0, on y -> S_3
- From S_2: on x -> S_3, on y -> S_0
- From S_3: on x -> S_2, on y -> S_1
Final states: S_1, S_3]

What language does this DFA recognize?
A. All strings of x and y which have either even number of x and even number of y or odd number of x and odd number of y
B. All strings of x and y which have equal number of x and y
C. All strings of x and y which have equal number of x and y and with either even number of x or odd number of y
D. All strings of x and y which have either even number of x and even number of y or odd number of x and even number of y
sourceAnswer: A. All strings of x and y which have either even number of x and even number of y or odd number of x and odd number of y

parsed.stem:
Consider the following DFA in which S_0 is the start state and S_1, S_3 are the final states.

[DFA with 4 states S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3 over alphabet {x, y}:
- From S_0: on x -> S_1, on y -> S_2
- From S_1: on x -> S_0, on y -> S_3
- From S_2: on x -> S_3, on y -> S_0
- From S_3: on x -> S_2, on y -> S_1
Final states: S_1, S_3]

What language does this DFA recognize?

options.text: All strings of x and y which have either even number of x and even number of y or odd number of x and odd number of y
All strings of x and y which have equal number of x and y
All strings of x and y which have equal number of x and y and with either even number of x or odd number of y
All strings of x and y which have either even number of x and even number of y or odd number of x and even number of y

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following finite automata P and Q over the alphabet {a, b, c}. The start states are indicated by a double arrow and final states are indicated by a double circle. Let the languages recognized by them be denoted by L(P) and L(Q) respectively.

[P: A 3-state NFA/DFA over {a,b,c} recognizing strings where some specific pattern holds.]
[Q: A 3-state NFA/DFA over {a,b,c} recognizing strings where some specific pattern holds.]

The automaton which recognizes the language L(P) ∩ L(Q) is:
A. [Automaton A]
B. [Automaton B]
C. [Automaton C]
D. [Automaton D]

parsed.stem:
Consider the following finite automata P and Q over the alphabet {a, b, c}. The start states are indicated by a double arrow and final states are indicated by a double circle. Let the languages recognized by them be denoted by L(P) and L(Q) respectively.

The automaton which recognizes the language L(P) ∩ L(Q) is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider the regular expression R = (a + b)*(aa + bb)(a + b)*

Which of the following non-deterministic finite automata recognizes the language defined by the regular expression R? Edges labeled lambda (lambda) denote transitions on the empty string.

A. [NFA A - shown in image]
B. [NFA B - shown in image]
C. [NFA C - shown in image]
D. [NFA D - shown in image]

parsed.stem:
Consider the regular expression R = (a + b)*(aa + bb)(a + b)*

Which of the following non-deterministic finite automata recognizes the language defined by the regular expression R? Edges labeled lambda (lambda) denote transitions on the empty string.

sourceQuestion:
If the final states and non-final states in the DFA below are interchanged, then which of the following languages over the alphabet {a, b} will be accepted by the new DFA?

(The DFA has: start state q0; on input 'a' from q0 go to q1, on input 'b' from q0 go to q0; on input 'a' from q1 go to q1, on input 'b' from q1 go to q2; on input 'a' from q2 go to q1, on input 'b' from q2 go to q0; final state is q2.)

A. Set of all strings that do not end with ab
B. Set of all strings that begin with either an a or b
C. Set of all strings that do not contain the substring ab
D. Set of all strings described by the regular expression b*(ab)* b*
sourceAnswer: A. Set of all strings that do not end with ab

parsed.stem:
If the final states and non-final states in the DFA below are interchanged, then which of the following languages over the alphabet {a, b} will be accepted by the new DFA?

(The DFA has: start state q0; on input 'a' from q0 go to q1, on input 'b' from q0 go to q0; on input 'a' from q1 go to q1, on input 'b' from q1 go to q2; on input 'a' from q2 go to q1, on input 'b' from q2 go to q0; final state is q2.)

options.text: Set of all strings that do not end with ab
Set of all strings that begin with either an a or b
Set of all strings that do not contain the substring ab
Set of all strings described by the regular expression b*(ab)* b*

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two finite automata. M1 accepts L1 and M2 accepts L2.

(M1 has two states p and q: start state p with self-loop on 'a', transition p -b-> q, self-loop on q with 'b', transition q -a-> p; both p and q are final states.)

(M2 has two states r and s: start state r, transition r -a-> s, transition s -a-> r, self-loop r -b-> r, self-loop s -b-> s; both r and s are final states.)

Which one of the following is/are TRUE?

A. L1 = L2
B. L1 subset L2
C. L1 intersection L2 = empty set
D. L1 union L2 = {a, b}*

parsed.stem:
Consider the following two finite automata. M1 accepts L1 and M2 accepts L2.

(M1 has two states p and q: start state p with self-loop on 'a', transition p -b-> q, self-loop on q with 'b', transition q -a-> p; both p and q are final states.)

(M2 has two states r and s: start state r, transition r -a-> s, transition s -a-> r, self-loop r -b-> r, self-loop s -b-> s; both r and s are final states.)

Which one of the following is/are TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a finite state machine (FSM) with one input X and one output f, represented by the given state transition table:

Present state | Next state (X=0) | Next state (X=1) | Output f (X=0) | Output f (X=1)
A             | F                | B                | 0              | 0
B             | D                | C                | 0              | 0
C             | F                | B                | 0              | 0
D             | G                | A                | 1              | 0
E             | D                | C                | 0              | 0
F             | F                | B                | 0              | 0
G             | G                | H                | 0              | 1
H             | G                | A                | 1              | 0

The minimum number of states required to realize this FSM is _______.

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6

parsed.stem:
Consider a finite state machine (FSM) with one input X and one output f, represented by the given state transition table:

Present state | Next state (X=0) | Next state (X=1) | Output f (X=0) | Output f (X=1)
A             | F                | B                | 0              | 0
B             | D                | C                | 0              | 0
C             | F                | B                | 0              | 0
D             | G                | A                | 1              | 0
E             | D                | C                | 0              | 0
F             | F                | B                | 0              | 0
G             | G                | H                | 0              | 1
H             | G                | A                | 1              | 0

The minimum number of states required to realize this FSM is _______.

sourceQuestion:
FORTRAN is a:
A. Regular language.
B. Context-free language.
C. Context-sensitive language.
D. None of the above.

parsed.stem: FORTRAN is a:

sourceQuestion:
If L1 is a context free language and L2 is a regular language, which of the following is/are false?
A. L1 - L2 is not context free
B. L1 ∩ L2 is context free
C. ~L1 is context free
D. ~L2 is regular

parsed.stem: If L1 is a context free language and L2 is a regular language, which of the following is/are false?

options.text: L1 - L2 is not context free

sourceQuestion:
Let L denote the languages generated by the grammar S -> 0S0 | 00. Which of the following is TRUE?
A. L = 0^+
B. L is regular but not 0^+
C. L is context free but not regular
D. L is not context free
sourceAnswer: B. L is regular but not 0^+

parsed.stem: Let L denote the languages generated by the grammar S -> 0S0 | 00. Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: L is context free but not regular

sourceQuestion:
The language accepted by a Pushdown Automaton in which the stack is limited to 10 items is best described as:
A. Context free
B. Regular
C. Deterministic Context free
D. Recursive

parsed.stem: The language accepted by a Pushdown Automaton in which the stack is limited to 10 items is best described as:

sourceQuestion:
The language {a^m b^n c^p | m, n, p >= 1} is:
A. regular
B. context-free but not regular
C. context-sensitive but not context free
D. type-0 but not context sensitive

parsed.stem: The language {a^m b^n c^p | m, n, p >= 1} is:

sourceQuestion:
Consider the languages: L1 = {a^p b^q c^q | p, q > 0} and L2 = {a^p b^q c^r | p, q, r > 0}. Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
A. L1 ∩ L2 is a context-free language
B. L1 ∪ L2 is a context-free language
C. L1 and L2 are context-free languages
D. L1 ∩ L2 is a context sensitive language
sourceAnswer: C. L1 and L2 are context-free languages

parsed.stem: Consider the languages: L1 = {a^p b^q c^q | p, q > 0} and L2 = {a^p b^q c^r | p, q, r > 0}. Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

options.text: L1 ∩ L2 is a context-free language
L1 ∪ L2 is a context-free language
L1 and L2 are context-free languages
L1 ∩ L2 is a context sensitive language

sourceQuestion:
For x in {0,1}^+, let d(x) denote the decimal value of x (e.g. d(101) = 5). Let L = {x in {0,1}^+ | d(x) mod 5 = 2 and d(x) mod 7 != 4}. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?
A. L is not accepted by a Turing Machine
B. L is regular
C. L is context-free but not regular
D. L is not context-free

parsed.stem: For x in {0,1}^+, let d(x) denote the decimal value of x (e.g. d(101) = 5). Let L = {x in {0,1}^+ | d(x) mod 5 = 2 and d(x) mod 7 != 4}. Which one of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: L is not accepted by a Turing Machine
L is context-free but not regular

sourceQuestion:
Let L_1 be a regular language, L_2 be a deterministic context-free language and L_3 be a recursively enumerable, but not recursive, language. Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. L_1 ∩ L_2 is a deterministic CFL
B. L_3 \ L_2 is recursive
C. L_1 ∪ L_2 is context free
D. L_1 ∩ L_2 ∩ L_3 is recursively enumerable

parsed.stem: Let L_1 be a regular language, L_2 be a deterministic context-free language and L_3 be a recursively enumerable, but not recursive, language. Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

options.text: L_1 ∩ L_2 is a deterministic CFL
L_1 ∩ L_2 ∩ L_3 is recursively enumerable

sourceQuestion:
The language L = {0^(2^i) | i >= 0} over the alphabet {0, 1} is:

A. not recursive
B. recursive and is a deterministic CFL
C. a regular language
D. not a deterministic CFL but a CFL
sourceAnswer: B. recursive and is a deterministic CFL

parsed.stem: The language L = {0^(2^i) | i >= 0} over the alphabet {0, 1} is:

options.text: recursive and is a deterministic CFL
not a deterministic CFL but a CFL

sourceQuestion:
Match the following:

Column P (Properties):
E. Checking that identifiers are declared before their use
F. Number of formal parameters in the declaration of a function agrees with the number of actual parameters in a call to that function
G. Arithmetic expressions with matched pairs of parentheses
H. Palindromes

Column Q (Language classes):
P. L = {a^(i) b^(j) c^(k) | i = j, k >= 1, m >= 1}
R. X -> XX | (X) | epsilon
S. X -> a^i b^(2i) | i >= 1

(As given in original: P corresponds to specific language constructs; match each property to the correct formal-language class)

A. E-P, F-R, G-Q, H-S
B. E-R, F-P, G-S, H-Q
C. E-Q, F-P, G-R, H-S
D. E-P, F-Q, G-R, H-S

parsed.stem:
Match the following:

Column P (Properties):
E. Checking that identifiers are declared before their use
F. Number of formal parameters in the declaration of a function agrees with the number of actual parameters in a call to that function
G. Arithmetic expressions with matched pairs of parentheses
H. Palindromes

Column Q (Language classes):
P. L = {a^(i) b^(j) c^(k) | i = j, k >= 1, m >= 1}
R. X -> XX | (X) | epsilon
S. X -> a^i b^(2i) | i >= 1

(As given in original: P corresponds to specific language constructs; match each property to the correct formal-language class)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is true for the language

{a^p | p is a prime}?

A. It is not accepted by a Turing Machine
B. It is regular but not context-free
C. It is context-free but not regular
D. It is neither regular nor context-free, but accepted by a Turing Machine
sourceAnswer: D. It is neither regular nor context-free, but accepted by a Turing Machine

parsed.stem:
Which of the following is true for the language

{a^p | p is a prime}?

options.text: It is not accepted by a Turing Machine
It is regular but not context-free
It is context-free but not regular
It is neither regular nor context-free, but accepted by a Turing Machine

sourceQuestion:
Let L = L1 ∩ L2, where L1 and L2 are languages as defined below:
L1 = {a^m b^n c^m | m, n >= 0}
L2 = {a^i b^j c^k | j > 0, k >= 0}
Then L is

A. Not recursive
B. Regular
C. Context free but not regular
D. Recursively enumerable but not context free
sourceAnswer: C. Context free but not regular

options.text: Recursively enumerable but not context free
Only L2 and L3 are context free.
Only L1 and L2 are context free.

sourceAnswer: B. L2 is a regular language.

options.text: Push Down Automata (PDA) can be used to recognize L1 and L2.
All the three languages are context free.
Turing machines can be used to recognize all the languages.

sourceAnswer: D. Complement of L0 is context-free but not regular.

options.text: Complement of L0 is context-free but not regular.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the context-free grammars over the alphabet {a, b, c} given below. S and T are non-terminals.
G_1 : S -> aSb | T,  T -> epsilon
G_2 : S -> aSa | bSb | T,  T -> epsilon
The language L(G_1) intersection L(G_2) is
A. Finite
B. Not finite but regular
C. Context-Free but not regular
D. Recursive but not context-free

parsed.stem:
Consider the context-free grammars over the alphabet {a, b, c} given below. S and T are non-terminals.
G_1 : S -> aSb | T,  T -> epsilon
G_2 : S -> aSa | bSb | T,  T -> epsilon
The language L(G_1) intersection L(G_2) is

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {a^p | p is prime}
L_2 = {a^n b^m c^(2m) | n >= 0, m >= 0}
L_3 = {a^n b^n c^(2n) | n >= 0}
L_4 = {a^n b^(2n) | n >= 0}
Which of the following are CORRECT?
I.   L_1 is context free but not regular
II.  L_2 is not context free
III. L_3 is not context free but recursive
IV.  L_4 is deterministic context free
A. I, II and IV only
B. II and III only
C. I and IV only
D. III and IV only

Consider the language L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | n != m} and the following statements.
I. L is deterministic context-free.
II. L is context-free but not deterministic context-free.
III. L is not LL(k) for any k.
Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?
A. I only
B. II only
C. I and III only
D. III only

parsed.stem:
Consider the language L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | n != m} and the following statements.
I. L is deterministic context-free.
II. L is context-free but not deterministic context-free.
III. L is not LL(k) for any k.
Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {uvuv | u, v in (0+1)*}
L_2 = {xy | x, y in (0+1)*, |x| = |y|, x != y}
Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. L_1 is regular and L_2 is context-free.
B. L_1 is context-free but not regular and L_2 is context-free.
C. Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free.
D. L_1 is context-free but L_2 is not context-free.
sourceAnswer: C. Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free.

options.text: L_1 is regular and L_2 is context-free.
L_1 is context-free but not regular and L_2 is context-free.
Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free.
L_1 is context-free but L_2 is not context-free.
